elements.sent-2411-001: The Casas--Alvero conjecture predicts that every univariate polynomial over a field of characteristic zero having a common factor with each of its derivatives $H_i(f)$ is a power of a linear polynomial.
elements.sent-2411-002: One approach to proving the conjecture is to first prove it for polynomials of some small degree $n$, compile a list of bad primes for that degree (namely, those primes $p$ for which the conjecture fails in degree $n$ and characteristic $p$) and then deduce the conjecture for all degrees of the form $np^\ell$, $\ell\in\N$, where $p$ is a good prime for $n$.
elements.sent-2411-003: In this paper we give an explicit description of the set of bad primes in any given degree $n$.
elements.sent-2411-004: In particular, we show that if the conjecture holds in degree $n$ then the bad primes for $n$ are bounded above by $\binom{\frac{n^2-n 2 {n-2 !\prod\limits_{i=1 ^{n-1 \binom{i+n-2 {n-2 ^{\binom{d-i+n-2 {n-2 }$.}}}}}}}}}
elements.sent-2411-005: In the year 2001 Eduardo Casas--Alvero published a paper on higher order polar germs of plane curve singularities \cite{C .}
elements.sent-2411-006: His work on polar germs inspired him to make the following conjecture.
elements.sent-2411-007: Let $K$ be a field, $f\in K[x]$ a non-constant monic univariate polynomial, $n:=\deg(f)$: \begin{equation}f=x^n+a_1x^{n-1}+\dots+a_n.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-008: Let \begin{equation}H_i(f)= \binom{n}{i}x^{n-i} + \binom{n-1}{i}a_1x^{n-i-1} + \cdots + \binom{i}{i}a_{n-i}\end{equation} be the $i$-th Hasse derivative of $f$.
elements.sent-2411-009: If $f \in K[x]$ is a Casas-Alvero polynomial of degree $n$, then there exists $b\in K$ such that $f(x) =(x-b)^n$.
elements.sent-2411-010: The simplest counterexample is the polynomial $f(x)= x^{p+1 -x^p$.}
elements.sent-2411-011: If the Casas--Alvero conjecture holds for the algebraic closure $\bar K$ of $K$ then it also holds for $K$ (the converse is not established, to our knowledge).
elements.sent-2411-012: Therefore, from now on we will assume that $K$ is algebraically closed.
elements.sent-2411-013: The following equivalences are known for each $n$ (\cite{DdJ , \cite{graf ): CA$_{n,0}$ holds $\iff$ CA$_{n,p}$ holds for some prime n \begin{deft A prime number $p$ is said to be a bad prime for $n$ if CA$_{n,p}$ is false.}}}

elements.sent-2411-019: Some integers cannot be written in the form $np^\ell$ where $p$ is a good prime for $n$, for example, \begin{equation}12=2^2 \cdot3,\ 20=2^2\cdot5,\ 24=2^3\cdot3,\ 28=2^2\cdot7,\ 30=2\cdot3\cdot5,\ 36=2^2\cdot3^2,\ 40=2^3\cdot5,\ldots\end{equation} CA$_{12,0}$ has been proved in \cite{CLO with the aid of a computer, by using a very clever strategy to cut down the computation of resultants and Gröbner bases.}

elements.sent-2411-024: For $j\in\{1,\dots,n-1\}$, let the involution \begin{equation}\Phi_j: K[x_1,\ldots,x_{n-1}] \to K[x_1,\ldots,x_{n-1}],\end{equation} be defined by \begin{equation}\Phi_j(x_i) =x_i-x_j \text{ for } i \neq j \text{ and } \Phi_j(x_j)=-x_j.\end{equation}.
